home.pageTitle: Compassionate Hospice Care in Los Angeles
home.metaDescription: Eminent Hospice Care delivers comfort-focused, dignified end-of-life care to patients and families in North Hollywood and across the greater Los Angeles area. Bilingual English & Korean care, available 24/7.

hero.heading:
Care With Dignity.
Comfort With Compassion.
hero.subtext: Eminent Hospice Care walks alongside patients and families in North Hollywood and across the greater Los Angeles area during life's most challenging moments — with skilled medical care, emotional support, and an unwavering commitment to dignity at home.
hero.caption: Medicare-certified · North Hollywood & Greater Los Angeles

philosophy.title: Care Focused on Comfort, Dignity, and Family
philosophy.paragraph1: Hospice is comfort-focused care for individuals with a life expectancy of six months or less. Rather than pursuing a cure, our team focuses on relieving pain, managing symptoms, and preserving dignity in the place you call home.
philosophy.paragraph2: We care for the whole person — body, mind, and spirit — and surround families with the practical, emotional, and spiritual support they need so no one has to walk this journey alone.
philosophy.marginalia: Dignity is not a luxury at the end of life — it is the standard we hold ourselves to, every visit.

levels.title: Four Levels of Care
levels.subtitle: Medicare defines four levels of hospice care to match each patient's needs as they change. We deliver every level, with care.

routine.title: Routine Home Care
routine.desc: Ongoing nursing visits, symptom management, and family support delivered wherever the patient calls home.

continuous.title: Continuous Home Care
continuous.desc: Around-the-clock nursing care during periods of acute symptom crisis, so the patient can stay home through difficult moments.

inpatient.title: General Inpatient Care
inpatient.desc: Inpatient stays (limited days) when pain or symptoms can no longer be safely managed at home.

respite.title: Respite Care
respite.desc: Up to five days of inpatient care that gives family caregivers time to rest and recharge.

team.title: Your Interdisciplinary Care Team
team.intro: Every patient is supported by a team of specialists who coordinate care, anticipate needs, and walk with the family through every step.

physician.name: Hospice Physician
physician.desc: Oversees the plan of care and manages complex medical needs in partnership with your primary doctor.

rn.name: Registered Nurse
rn.desc: Leads symptom management, medication oversight, and family education at each visit.

lvn.name: Licensed Vocational Nurse
lvn.desc: Provides bedside nursing care, comfort measures, and continuity between RN visits.

msw.name: Medical Social Worker
msw.desc: Helps families navigate emotional, financial, and community-resource concerns.

chaplain.name: Spiritual Care / Chaplain
chaplain.desc: Offers non-denominational spiritual support honoring the patient's faith and tradition.

aide.name: Hospice Aide
aide.desc: Assists with bathing, grooming, and daily personal care with gentleness and respect.

bereavement.name: Bereavement Coordinator
bereavement.desc: Supports the family for up to thirteen months following the loss of a loved one.

volunteer.name: Trained Volunteer
volunteer.desc: Provides companionship, light errands, and respite presence so caregivers can step away.

area.title: Service Area
area.desc: Serving North Hollywood and families across the greater Los Angeles area — from the San Fernando Valley to surrounding communities.

languages.title: Languages
languages.desc: English and 한국어 — culturally aware care for Korean-American families.

coverage.title: Coverage
coverage.desc: Medicare Part A, Medi-Cal, and most private insurance plans accepted.

testimonial.quote: My father passed away from advanced Parkinson's disease and was under hospice care with Eminent for quite some time. The nurses and staff at Eminent were some of the kindest and most compassionate people I have ever met. They were also incredibly efficient, professional, and excellent communicators (I could text them even at night or on weekends and get a response right away). They also provide seamless bilingual care — speaking Korean with my father while communicating clearly with me in English. They treated my father and our family with such warmth, dignity, and respect throughout the entire process. The care we received was so exceptional that after my father's passing, I felt compelled to leave a public review for this amazing hospice service. If you or a loved one need hospice care with bilingual support, I cannot recommend Eminent highly enough.

finalCta.title: Available 24/7 to Support Your Family
finalCta.subtitle: Speak with a member of our care team any hour of the day. There is no obligation — only listening, information, and care.

common.educationalDisclaimer: This information is general education, not legal or insurance advice. Coverage decisions are made by Medicare, Medi-Cal, and your physician. For your specific situation, please contact us or your insurance provider.
common.medicalDisclaimer: This information is general education and is not a substitute for medical advice from your hospice team. For questions about your loved one's symptoms, medications, or care, call your hospice nurse anytime — day or night.
common.sidebarContactBody: We answer questions, verify coverage, and start referrals — no obligation.

pageBottomCta.title: Have Questions About Hospice?
pageBottomCta.subtitle: Our care team can answer your questions, verify your coverage, or help you start a referral. There's no obligation — only listening and information.

understandingHospice.metaDescription: What hospice care is, who qualifies, common myths corrected, and what to expect after enrollment. A plain-language guide for patients and families in North Hollywood and the greater Los Angeles area.
understandingHospice.introParagraph: Hospice is one of the most misunderstood parts of modern medicine. This page is a plain-language guide to what hospice actually is, who qualifies, and what happens after you enroll.

whatIsHospice.title: What Is Hospice?
whatIsHospice.p1: Hospice is comfort-focused care for patients facing a serious, life-limiting illness when curative treatments are no longer effective or no longer wanted. Rather than trying to cure the illness, hospice focuses on relieving pain, managing symptoms, and supporting the patient's emotional, spiritual, and family well-being.
whatIsHospice.p2: Hospice is a benefit you elect — you choose comfort care as your goal, and an interdisciplinary team comes to wherever you call home to deliver it. Most patients receive hospice at home (some in a skilled nursing facility or assisted-living community); care can also happen in an assisted living community, skilled nursing facility, or a hospice inpatient setting.

whoQualifies.title: Who Qualifies?
whoQualifies.intro: To enroll in hospice under Medicare or Medi-Cal, two physicians — typically your attending physician and the hospice medical director — must certify that your illness is terminal, meaning a life expectancy of six months or less if the illness runs its normal course. You sign a statement choosing the hospice benefit, and you can revoke that choice at any time.

cancer.name: Cancer
cancer.desc: Advanced cancers where curative treatment has been discontinued or is no longer effective.

heart.name: Advanced Heart Disease
heart.desc: End-stage heart failure or other progressive cardiac conditions.

copd.name: COPD & Lung Disease
copd.desc: Advanced chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and other end-stage respiratory illnesses.

dementia.name: Alzheimer's & Dementia
dementia.desc: Advanced dementia where the patient can no longer communicate meaningfully or perform basic daily activities.

als.name: ALS & Neurological Disease
als.desc: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Parkinson's, and other progressive neurological conditions.

kidney.name: End-Stage Kidney or Liver Disease
kidney.desc: Kidney failure with discontinued dialysis, or advanced liver disease.

whoQualifies.prognosisNote: A six-month prognosis is a clinical estimate, not a deadline. Many patients live longer than expected once symptoms are well managed. If you continue to need hospice beyond six months, your hospice physician can re-certify you for additional benefit periods.

myths.title: Common Myths About Hospice
myths.intro: These are the misconceptions we hear most often. Each one keeps families from getting care that could help them.

items.fact: No — hospice is not giving up; it is finding quality of life for the days that remain. Hospice is choosing comfort and dignity, not death. Many patients feel relief, regain their appetite, and find time for what matters most once aggressive treatments are set aside.

No. Research consistently shows hospice patients often live as long as, or longer than, similar patients who do not enroll. Better symptom control, fewer hospitalizations, and emotional support all contribute.

items.myth: You have to stop all your medications.
items.fact: No. Hospice continues medications that relieve symptoms — pain, nausea, anxiety, shortness of breath. Only treatments aimed at curing the terminal illness are paused; comfort medications are not. Patients often continue medications that maintain quality of life — for example for high blood pressure or diabetes — when medically safe.

items.myth: Hospice is only for cancer patients.
items.fact: No. Any terminal illness with a six-month prognosis qualifies — including heart disease, lung disease, dementia, kidney failure, ALS, and many others.

items.myth: Hospice is only for the very last days.
items.fact: No. Hospice is most beneficial when started early — weeks or months before the end of life, not days. Earlier enrollment means more time for symptom control, family preparation, and meaningful moments together.

whatToExpect.title: What to Expect After You Enroll
whatToExpect.p1: An RN case manager visits to complete an initial assessment, set up the care plan with you, and arrange any medications or equipment you need. Most equipment — hospital beds, oxygen, wheelchairs, commodes — is delivered to your home at no cost.
whatToExpect.p2: Your interdisciplinary team — physician, nurses, hospice aide, medical social worker, chaplain, and bereavement coordinator — meets every two weeks to review your care plan. RN visits are typically one to three times per two weeks, with aide and other team members on a schedule that fits your needs. A nurse is on call 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.
whatToExpect.p3: The plan of care is yours. If something isn't working, tell us — we adjust. If a symptom gets worse, call the on-call nurse.

whereCareHappens.title: Where Hospice Happens
whereCareHappens.intro: You choose where you receive care. The hospice team comes to you.

home.name: At Home
home.desc: Most hospice care happens in the patient's home — house, apartment, or family member's home — wherever feels familiar and safe.

assistedLiving.name: Assisted Living
assistedLiving.desc: Hospice teams come to assisted living communities to deliver the same level of care as we would in a private home.

snf.name: Skilled Nursing Facility
snf.desc: If you live in a skilled nursing facility, hospice care can be delivered there. Medi-Cal additionally covers room and board for qualifying patients.

inpatient.name: Inpatient Hospice
inpatient.desc: For short periods of acute symptom management, care can shift to an inpatient hospice setting until the patient is stable enough to return home. (Limited availability depending on service area.)

stoppingHospice.title: You Can Stop or Pause Hospice
stoppingHospice.p1: Electing hospice is not a one-way door. If you decide to pursue curative treatment again, you can revoke the hospice benefit at any time and return to standard Medicare or your other insurance. You can also re-elect hospice later if your situation changes. There is no penalty and no waiting period. That said, Medicare often reviews revocation cases to confirm eligibility, so revocation should be considered carefully.

hospiceLaws.metaDescription: Federal Conditions of Participation, your rights as a hospice patient, California advance directives, and how to file a complaint. A plain-language guide.
hospiceLaws.introParagraph: Hospice is one of the most heavily regulated parts of American healthcare — and that's a good thing. The standards below exist to protect you. This page explains them in plain language.

cops.title: Federal Standards: Conditions of Participation
cops.body: Every hospice that accepts Medicare must meet the federal Conditions of Participation. These cover patient rights, assessment of each patient's needs, the interdisciplinary care team, the quality of care, infection control, and how a hospice is organized and run. The standards are set and enforced by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS), and hospices are surveyed periodically to confirm compliance.
cops.cmsLinkLabel: Read the full regulation (42 CFR Part 418) →

patientRights.title: Your Rights as a Hospice Patient
patientRights.intro: The following rights are paraphrased from 42 CFR §418.52 — the federal Patient Rights condition of participation. They apply to every patient of every Medicare-certified hospice.
items: To receive effective pain management and symptom relief.
To be treated with dignity and respect, no matter who you are.
To participate in developing and changing your plan of care.
To choose your attending physician.
To privacy and confidentiality of your medical information, under HIPAA.
To refuse care or treatment without fear of consequences.
To voice grievances about your care without retaliation.
To be informed of services and any costs in advance, before they are provided.
To receive care without discrimination based on race, religion, national origin, gender, sexual orientation, gender identity, age, or disability.
To have your cultural background, language preferences, and personal values honored.
patientRights.linkLabel: Read the official Patient Rights condition (42 CFR §418.52) →

advanceDirectives.title: Advance Directives
advanceDirectives.intro: An advance directive is a way to make your wishes known ahead of time, in case you cannot speak for yourself. In California, there are three main forms to know about.

livingWill.title: Living Will
livingWill.body: A living will is a written record of the kinds of medical treatment you want — or don't want — if you are unable to communicate. In California, any adult can complete an Advance Health Care Directive that combines a living will with a healthcare power of attorney.
livingWill.caLawNote: California Probate Code §4670 et seq. governs Advance Health Care Directives. Free forms are available from the California Hospital Association and the California Department of Justice.

dpoa.title: Durable Power of Attorney for Healthcare (DPOA-HC)
dpoa.body: A DPOA-HC names someone you trust — a spouse, adult child, friend — to make medical decisions for you when you cannot. In California, this is part of the same Advance Health Care Directive form. You can change or revoke the appointment at any time while you are able to do so.

polst.title: POLST — Physician Orders for Life-Sustaining Treatment
polst.body: POLST is different from a living will: it is an actual medical order, signed by a physician (or nurse practitioner or physician assistant), that travels with the patient across care settings. It is intended for people who are seriously ill or have a life-limiting condition. POLST records specific decisions — such as whether to attempt resuscitation, whether to transfer to a hospital, and what level of medical intervention to use.
polst.paperColorNote: In California, the POLST form is recommended to be printed on bright pink (Ultra Pink) paper so emergency responders can locate it quickly. The form is legally valid on any color paper or in electronic form.

complaints.title: How to File a Complaint or Grievance
complaints.intro: If you believe you are not receiving the care you are entitled to, you have several options. You can use any or all of them.

eminent.name: Eminent Hospice Care directly
eminent.contact: Direct line: (818) 824-3702 (24/7). Text-only: (213) 340-4429 (24/7). After business hours (Monday–Friday, 9 AM – 5 PM), our on-call nurse responds.

cdph.name: California Department of Public Health (CDPH), Licensing & Certification
cdph.contact: (800) 228-1019 — open Monday through Friday, 8:00 AM to 5:00 PM Pacific.

medicare.name: Medicare Beneficiary Ombudsman
medicare.contact: 1-800-MEDICARE (1-800-633-4227) — available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.

complaints.retaliationProtection: Federal law protects you: filing a complaint in good faith cannot result in retaliation, reduction of services, or discharge from hospice care.

hipaa.title: HIPAA & Your Privacy
hipaa.body: Your medical information is protected under the federal Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA). We may use and share your information only as needed to deliver your care, coordinate with other providers, obtain payment, and comply with the law. We will not sell your information, and we will not share it for marketing without your written permission.
hipaa.noticeLabel: Our full Notice of Privacy Practices will be published before launch. The complete notice will be linked from the website footer when available.

insurance.metaDescription: How Medicare Part A, Medi-Cal, and private insurance cover hospice care. FY2026 federal rates, the four levels of care, and step-by-step election process.
insurance.introParagraph: Hospice care is covered in full for most patients. This page explains how Medicare, Medi-Cal, and private insurance handle hospice, plus the step-by-step election process.

cost.title: Quick Answer: What Does Hospice Cost?
cost.p1: For most patients, hospice care costs $0. Medicare Part A and Medi-Cal cover hospice services in full when you elect the hospice benefit. There are no deductibles or coinsurance for hospice services themselves.
cost.footnote: Small Medicare cost-sharing applies in two narrow cases: outpatient drugs related to your terminal illness (up to $5 per prescription) and inpatient respite care (5% of the Medicare-approved rate, capped at the annual inpatient hospital deductible). Both caps are set by federal regulation; you will never be billed more than these limits. The patient or family will be notified of any additional cost by the hospice agency.

medicare.title: Medicare Part A Hospice Benefit
medicare.eligibility: To qualify, you must be entitled to Medicare Part A. Two physicians — your attending physician and the hospice medical director — must certify that your illness is terminal, with a life expectancy of six months or less if the illness runs its normal course.
medicare.covered: Medicare covers all hospice services delivered by your hospice team: nursing visits, physician oversight, hospice aide care, social worker, chaplain, bereavement support, medications related to your terminal illness, durable medical equipment, and all four levels of care described below.
medicare.notCovered: Medicare hospice does not cover treatments aimed at curing the terminal illness once you elect hospice, room and board in your private home, or care from providers outside your hospice team unless arranged in advance. Room and board in a nursing facility may be covered separately by Medi-Cal.

levels.title: The Four Levels of Care
levels.intro: Medicare defines four levels of hospice care. Your level can change day-to-day depending on what you need. Eminent delivers all four.
levels.sourceNote: Source: CMS FY2026 Hospice Wage Index Final Rule (CMS-1835-F), effective October 1, 2025. Federal rates are updated annually each October.
levels.fy2026Note: Federal payment rates increased 2.6% in FY2026. Specific dollar amounts below reflect the FY2026 final rule.

rhc.name: Routine Home Care (RHC)
rhc.desc: The most common level. Scheduled visits from your hospice team to wherever you live.
rhc.rateNote: Paid at a tiered daily rate, with a lower rate after day 60 to encourage sustained engagement throughout the hospice stay.

chc.name: Continuous Home Care (CHC)
chc.desc: Eight or more hours of mostly nursing care in a 24-hour period, during a short crisis of pain or symptoms — delivered in your home so you do not have to go to a hospital.
chc.rateNote: Paid at an hourly rate. Used for brief, intensive periods, not for sustained nursing.

gip.name: General Inpatient Care (GIP)
gip.desc: Short-term inpatient care for symptoms that cannot be safely managed at home. Delivered in a hospital, skilled nursing facility, or hospice inpatient unit.
gip.rateNote: Paid at a daily facility rate.

irc.name: Inpatient Respite Care (IRC)
irc.desc: Up to five consecutive days of inpatient care, used to give the family caregiver a planned break.
irc.rateNote: Paid at a daily rate. A 5% patient coinsurance applies, capped at the annual inpatient hospital deductible.

aggregateCap.label: FY2026 Aggregate Cap
aggregateCap.desc: The federal cap on average Medicare payments to a hospice per patient over a year. This is a backstop on the program, not a per-patient limit on the care you can receive.

medical.title: Medi-Cal Hospice Coverage
medical.body: Medi-Cal covers hospice services for California residents who qualify for Medi-Cal benefits. Coverage parallels Medicare's hospice benefit and is delivered by the same Medicare-certified hospices.
medical.roomAndBoardNote: Important: for patients living in a skilled nursing facility, Medi-Cal pays a room-and-board amount of at least 95% of the facility's Medi-Cal nursing-facility rate. This is the meaningful difference between Medi-Cal and Medicare hospice — Medicare alone does not pay for room and board.

comparison.title: Medicare vs. Medi-Cal vs. Private Insurance
comparison.intro: A side-by-side look at what each pays for. For most patients in North Hollywood and the greater Los Angeles area, Medicare or Medi-Cal will be the primary coverage.
comparison.tableCaption: Hospice coverage comparison across the three most common payers.

election.title: The Election Process — Step by Step
election.intro: Electing hospice takes one signed form. Here is how it usually goes.
steps: Your attending physician and the hospice medical director both certify that your illness is terminal, with a life expectancy of six months or less.
You sign the hospice election statement, choosing comfort care for your terminal illness.
You name your attending physician and choose your hospice provider.
Within 48 hours, the hospice team visits to set up your care plan and arrange medications or equipment.
You have two 90-day benefit periods, then unlimited 60-day periods. Each requires a recertification that your illness is still terminal.
You can revoke the hospice benefit at any time and return to standard insurance. You can also re-elect later. There is no penalty and no waiting period.
election.revocationNote: Revoking is your right. Many patients do not — but the option is always there.

help.title: Need Help Verifying Coverage?
help.body: Our admissions team verifies your Medicare, Medi-Cal, or private insurance coverage at no cost and with no obligation. We can also help you understand what your specific plan covers before you make any decisions.

about.metaDescription: Eminent Hospice Care delivers compassionate, culturally aware end-of-life care across North Hollywood and the greater Los Angeles area. Learn our mission, values, interdisciplinary team, and bilingual EN/KO commitment to Korean-American families.
about.introParagraph: Eminent Hospice Care is a Medicare-certified hospice serving North Hollywood and the greater Los Angeles area with a particular commitment to bilingual care for Korean-American families. We exist for one reason: to walk alongside patients and the people who love them through life's final chapter.

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.body: Our mission is to walk alongside patients and families through life's final chapter — with skilled medical care, gentle presence, and respect for the language, traditions, and choices that make each family who they are. We deliver every level of hospice care defined by Medicare, in the place each person calls home, twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week.

values.title: Our Values
values.intro: Four commitments shape every visit, every conversation, every plan of care we write.

compassion.title: Compassion
compassion.body: We meet every patient and family where they are, with patience and gentleness. We listen first.

dignity.title: Dignity
dignity.body: We protect each person's right to be seen, heard, and treated with respect — at every visit, in every decision.

culturalHumility.title: Cultural Humility
culturalHumility.body: We listen to each family's language, faith, and traditions, and we let those guide care rather than impose our own.

clinicalExcellence.title: Clinical Excellence
clinicalExcellence.body: We hold ourselves to the federal Conditions of Participation — and to the higher bar of what families would want for their own.

story.title: A Brief Story
story.body: Founded in 2018, Eminent Hospice Care provides an individualized program of physical, emotional, spiritual, and psychosocial care for people in the last phases of a life-limiting illness, with an emphasis on control of pain and other symptoms. Under the leadership of current owner Hanna Cha, our team works to alleviate as much pain, worry, and inconvenience as possible for patients and families facing serious illness — with a particular commitment to the Korean-American community of greater Los Angeles, whose families have long needed care delivered in their own language.

serviceArea.title: Service Area
serviceArea.body: We serve North Hollywood and the greater Los Angeles area, with patients and families across the region. Our team coordinates visits, on-call coverage, and medication delivery anywhere within our service area.
serviceArea.hoursNote: Available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week — a registered nurse answers our on-call line at every hour.

culturalCompetence.title: Care for Korean-American Families
culturalCompetence.intro: Hospice decisions are hard in any language. Doing them in your second language is harder. Eminent's bilingual team and Korean-American cultural awareness exist to make this easier.
culturalCompetence.koreanIntro: 에미넌트 호스피스 케어는 한국어로 진행되는 입원 상담, 한국어를 구사하는 케어 팀, 그리고 한인 가족의 전통과 신앙을 존중하는 케어를 제공합니다.

bullets.bilingual: Intake, care-plan conversations, and on-call support available in English and Korean.
bullets.koreanStaff: Nearly all of our team members speak Korean — clinical, admissions, and on-call support — so families can have every conversation in their preferred language.
bullets.traditions: We learn each family's traditions around illness, end of life, and remembrance — and let those guide care.
bullets.chaplainCoord: Our chaplain coordinates with the family's own faith tradition; we do not impose one.

culturalCompetence.note: If you would like your first conversation to be in Korean, just say so when you call — we will route you to a Korean-speaking team member.

team.title: Your Care Team
team.intro: Every patient is supported by an interdisciplinary team — a physician, registered nurse, licensed vocational nurse, medical social worker, chaplain, hospice aide, bereavement coordinator, and trained volunteers. Each role is described in detail on our Services page.
team.viewServicesLink: See how each team member supports your care →

items.certified: Medicare-certified and surveyed against the federal Conditions of Participation.
items.sameDay: Same-day admission when clinically appropriate — calls placed in the morning often have an RN at the home by evening.
items.pharmacy: Pharmacy and durable medical equipment (DME) coordinated by our team — comfort medications and equipment arrive at the home, not at the family's errand list.
items.oncall: A registered nurse answers our 24/7 on-call line at every hour.
items.bilingual: English and Korean intake, with cultural awareness woven into the care plan, not bolted on at the end.

aboutJsonLd.name: Eminent Hospice Care Inc.
aboutJsonLd.areaServed: North Hollywood and the greater Los Angeles area, CA

services.metaDescription: All four Medicare-defined levels of hospice care, an eight-discipline interdisciplinary team, 24/7 on-call nursing, and medication + equipment delivery — delivered throughout North Hollywood and the greater Los Angeles area, in English and Korean.
services.introParagraph: Hospice is a comprehensive benefit, not a single service. This page walks through every level of care we deliver, every discipline on our team, and the supporting services that make care work day-to-day at home.

offer.title: What We Offer
offer.body: All four Medicare-defined levels of hospice care, delivered by a full interdisciplinary team, twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week, anywhere a patient calls home in North Hollywood and the greater Los Angeles area. New to hospice?
offer.linkToUnderstanding: Start with our Understanding Hospice Care guide →

levels.title: The Four Levels of Care
levels.intro: Your level of care can change day to day depending on what you need. We deliver every level, with care. For coverage and payment details, see our Insurance & Medicare page.

routine.title: Routine Home Care
routine.summary: Scheduled visits from your hospice team — nursing, aide, social worker, chaplain — to wherever you live. The most common level, used by most patients most of the time.
routine.clinical: Routine Home Care is appropriate when symptoms are stable and the patient and family can manage day-to-day at home with scheduled team support. This is the level most hospice patients use most of the time.
routine.atHome: Visit frequencies are individualized: typically a skilled nursing visit one to three times per week, an aide several times per week, and the social worker and chaplain on a schedule that fits the family's needs. Visits change as the patient changes.

continuous.title: Continuous Home Care
continuous.summary: Nursing presence at home during an acute symptom crisis, so the patient can stay home through a difficult stretch instead of going to the hospital.
continuous.clinical: Continuous Home Care provides nursing presence for stretches of eight or more hours within a twenty-four-hour period during an acute symptom crisis — pain that is not yet controlled, severe shortness of breath, or another situation that would otherwise lead to a hospital visit. The goal is to manage the crisis at home.
continuous.atHome: An RN (and often an LVN or other staff) stays at the home until the symptom is brought under control. Hours and staffing vary depending on symptoms and the patient's needs. Once the crisis is resolved, care returns to the Routine Home Care level.

gip.title: General Inpatient Care
gip.summary: Short-term inpatient care for symptoms that cannot be safely managed at home. Patients return home as soon as symptoms are stable.
gip.clinical: General Inpatient Care is short-term care at a contracted facility — hospital, freestanding hospice inpatient unit, or skilled nursing facility — used when symptoms cannot be safely managed at home. Patients return home as soon as symptoms are stable.
gip.atHome: We coordinate the transfer, communicate with the facility's clinical team, and stay involved during the inpatient stay.

respite.title: Inpatient Respite Care
respite.summary: Up to five consecutive days of inpatient stay so the primary family caregiver can rest. The patient is cared for in a Medicare-certified facility; the family steps away knowing the patient is safe.
respite.clinical: Respite Care provides up to five consecutive days of inpatient stay to give the primary family caregiver a needed break. The patient is cared for in a Medicare-certified facility; the family rests.
respite.atHome: Respite is planned, not reactive — families schedule it in advance. We arrange the bed, the transfer, and the return home. Caregivers come back rested and the patient's care does not skip a beat.

levels.costFootnote: For coverage and what families pay, see our <link>Insurance & Medicare page</link>.

team.title: Your Interdisciplinary Team
team.intro: Every patient is supported by a team of specialists who coordinate, anticipate, and walk with the family through every step. Each discipline below has a clear, specific role.

physician.body: Oversees the plan of care in partnership with the patient's attending physician. Manages complex medical needs — uncontrolled symptoms, medication adjustments, decisions that require physician sign-off. Available to the team around the clock.

rn.body: The clinical lead. Conducts the initial assessment, sets up the care plan with the patient and family, and leads symptom management at every subsequent visit. Typical visit frequency is one to three times per week, with more visits during periods of change. The on-call line (English and Korean) is answered by an RN twenty-four hours a day — by direct call or text.

lvn.body: Provides bedside nursing care, comfort measures, and continuity between RN visits. Works under the RN's plan of care, with regular communication so changes the LVN sees at the bedside reach the rest of the team quickly.

msw.body: Helps families navigate the parts of hospice that are not strictly medical — emotional support, family meetings, advance-directive paperwork, community-resource referrals (transportation, meal services, financial, insurance, and social-services (IHSS) assistance), and care coordination with skilled nursing facilities. Visits on a schedule that fits the family's needs.

chaplain.body: Offers non-denominational spiritual support honoring the patient's faith and tradition. Coordinates with the family's own clergy when that is preferred. The chaplain is available for ritual, conversation, or simply presence — whichever the family wants. Generally, chaplains visit the patient about once a month.

aide.body: Assists with bathing, grooming, light meal preparation, and daily personal care — the day-to-day tasks that become harder as illness progresses. Visit frequency is set by the RN and tailored to the family's needs. Generally, hospice aides visit the patient about twice a week.

bereavement.body: Our bereavement coordinator stays in touch with surviving family for thirteen months after the loss — calls, support-group resources, anniversary check-ins. Grief support is not an add-on to hospice; it is hospice.

volunteer.body: Provides companionship, light errands, and respite presence so caregivers can step away briefly. Our volunteers complete a structured training program and are background-checked — the role is meaningful, not casual.

additional.title: Additional Services
additional.intro: The services that make hospice work day-to-day at home.

oncall.title: 24/7 On-Call Nursing Line
oncall.body: A registered nurse answers our on-call line at every hour. If a symptom worsens or a family member is worried, calling or texting us is always the right first step.

meds.title: Medication Delivery
meds.body: All medications related to the terminal illness are coordinated by our team and delivered to the home or residence. Families do not chase prescriptions or stand in line at the pharmacy.

dme.title: Durable Medical Equipment (DME)
dme.body: Hospital bed, oxygen concentrator, wheelchair, commode, and other equipment are ordered and delivered to the home or residence at no additional cost when ordered through our hospice.

bereavement.title: Bereavement Support
bereavement.body: Thirteen months of follow-up for surviving family — calls, support-group resources, anniversary check-ins. Available to spouses, adult children, and any family member who participated in the patient's care.

volunteer.title: Volunteer Program
volunteer.body: Companionship, light errands, and respite presence — trained and background-checked. Families can request a volunteer through our social worker.

howToStart.title: How to Start
howToStart.intro: Three steps. Usually a same-day or next-day start.

steps.1: Call our 24/7 line or use the Contact form. A team member answers.
steps.2: We coordinate with the patient's physician to certify eligibility — most patients can be certified within hours (or up to a few days, depending on the physician's availability).
steps.3: An RN visits within 48 hours (often same-day) to evaluate for hospice eligibility and set up the care plan and arrange medications and equipment.

forFamilies.metaDescription: Practical guidance for families caring for a loved one on hospice — what to expect, home-care basics, the dying process, caregiver wellness, and bereavement support.
forFamilies.introParagraph: Caring for a loved one on hospice is one of the most meaningful and most demanding things a family can do. This page is orientation — what to expect, the practical basics, and how to take care of yourself along the way. Your hospice nurse is always your first call for anything specific to your loved one.

expect.title: What to Expect When Your Loved One Begins Hospice
expect.body1: In the first few days — admission scheduled with the family and/or patient — a hospice nurse visits to review the plan of care with you, set up medications and equipment, and explain how to reach us 24/7. The pace is intentionally unhurried — we want you to feel oriented, not rushed.
expect.body2: Visits then settle into a rhythm tailored to your loved one's needs. Most families will see the nurse twice a week, the aide more often for hygiene help, and the social worker and chaplain on a regular basis, as scheduled. As needs change, the team adjusts.

items.adminVisit: An admission visit from a registered nurse to review the care plan, answer questions, and orient you and your loved one to hospice.
items.carePlan: A written plan of care reflecting your loved one's goals, the family's preferences, and the team's clinical assessment.
items.medsDme: Comfort medications and any needed equipment (hospital bed, oxygen, wheelchair) and supplies (incontinence supplies, or extra nourishment such as Ensure or Glucerna) coordinated and delivered to the home.
items.oncallLine: A 24/7 nurse line activated for any question, day or night.
items.idgCadence: The interdisciplinary team — nurse, aide, social worker, chaplain — introduced and visit schedules set.

basics.title: Caring at Home — The Practical Basics
basics.intro: Your hospice nurse will teach you what you need to know at the bedside, and the team is on call 24/7 for anything that comes up. The notes below are orientation, not a substitute for that teaching.

medications.title: Medications
medications.body: Comfort medications are organized in a kit and delivered to the home. The nurse walks you through what each medication is for, when to give it, and how to record what you give. Keep medications in a cool, dry place out of reach of children.
medications.neverGiveNote: Never give a medication you have not been instructed to give. When you're unsure, call the on-call nurse first — that's what the line is for.

oxygen.title: Oxygen Concentrator
oxygen.body: If your loved one needs oxygen, a concentrator is delivered with tubing and a nasal cannula. The nurse will set the flow rate; you do not need to adjust it.
oxygen.safetyNote: Keep flames, smoking, and flammables at least 5 feet away from the concentrator and tubing. Do not use petroleum-based products (Vaseline, oil-based lotions) around the cannula.
oxygen.powerOutageNote: Power outage: small portable tanks are typically provided as a backup. Confirm the backup plan with the nurse at admission and call the on-call line if you are unsure.
oxygen.bedsideTeachingNote: Detailed device handling is taught at the bedside by the nurse — this page is orientation only.

equipment.title: Equipment (Hospital Bed, Commode, Wheelchair)
equipment.body: Equipment is delivered, set up, and serviced by the hospice's medical-supply partner. If something breaks or stops working, call our office — we will arrange replacement or repair.

hygiene.title: Hygiene & Skin Care
hygiene.body: Hospice aides assist with bathing, dressing, and personal care on a regular schedule. Between visits, gentle skin care and repositioning your loved one about every two hours helps prevent pressure injuries. Mouth care — moistening the lips and inside of the mouth — becomes especially important as drinking decreases.

eating.title: Eating & Drinking Changes
eating.body: Appetite and thirst naturally decrease near the end of life. Your loved one may want only sips of water, a favorite taste, or nothing at all. This is a normal part of the body slowing down, not suffering from hunger or thirst.
eating.gentleNote: Forcing food or fluids can cause distress, choking, or fluid in the lungs. Offer small sips, ice chips, or favorite tastes when your loved one wants them, and follow their lead.

dyingProcess.title: Understanding the Dying Process
dyingProcess.intro: This is the hardest section to read. Many families come back to it days or weeks later. Skip ahead if you need to — the sidebar lets you jump past it.
dyingProcess.gentleNote: The order and timing of these changes vary. Not every person experiences every sign, and some signs come and go. This is general guidance, not a prediction. Your hospice nurse can talk through what you are observing.

signs.1: Sleeping more during the day and waking less easily.
signs.2: Withdrawing from social activity, conversation, or favorite pastimes.
signs.3: Eating and drinking less; gradual weight loss.
signs.4: More fatigue and less energy for movement.

signs.1: Eating and drinking very little or stopping altogether.
signs.2: Sleeping most of the time and being harder to wake.
signs.3: Changes in breathing pattern — sometimes faster, sometimes slower, with pauses.
signs.4: Less consistent response to voice and touch.
signs.5: Hands and feet may feel cool; skin color may begin to change.

hours.title: In the final hours

signs.1: Irregular breathing with longer pauses between breaths.
signs.2: Audible congestion in the chest or throat — the family often hears it more than the patient feels it.
signs.3: Color changes in the skin, particularly around the lips, hands, and feet.
signs.4: Eyes may remain partly open; response may cease.

hours.hearingNote: Hearing is widely believed to remain longest. Speaking softly and saying what you want to say is always appropriate.

dyingProcess.variabilityNote: If you see something new or worrying — a sudden change in breathing, distress, a fall, anything that feels urgent — call the on-call nurse. We can talk through it, visit if needed, and stay on the line.

symptoms.title: Managing Pain & Symptoms — When to Call
symptoms.body: When in doubt, call the on-call nurse. It is always the right call. We can talk you through what you're seeing, adjust the care plan, or come out for an in-person visit.

items.1: New or worsening pain not relieved by the prescribed medication.
items.2: Shortness of breath, choking, or difficult breathing.
items.4: Vomiting, especially if it cannot be kept down.
items.5: Fever, signs of infection, or unusual bleeding.
items.6: A fall, even if there is no obvious injury.
items.7: Any sudden change in condition that worries you.

symptoms.do_not_911_note: Please do not call 911 unless you intend to revoke the hospice election. A 911 transport often leads to a hospital admission and curative treatment, which conflicts with the hospice benefit. For symptoms, our 24/7 nurse line is the right first call — we will help.

wellness.title: Caring for Yourself — Caregiver Wellness
wellness.body: Caregiving is exhausting work. Recognizing the signs of burnout — without judgment — and asking for help early is the most important thing you can do, both for yourself and for your loved one.

items.1: Persistent exhaustion that sleep does not fix.
items.2: Trouble sleeping or sleeping too much.
items.4: Withdrawing from friends, family, or activities you enjoy.
items.5: Getting sick more often than usual.
items.6: Feelings of resentment, guilt, or numbness.

askForHelp.title: Ask for help
askForHelp.body: Help is built into hospice. Some specific options to ask the team about:
askForHelp.respiteLinkLabel: Respite Care — up to five days of inpatient stay so you can rest
askForHelp.volunteerLinkLabel: Trained volunteers — companionship, short errands, a presence in the home
askForHelp.socialWorkerNote: Our social worker offers caregiver counseling and can help connect you with community support and faith-based resources.

bereavement.title: After the Loss — Bereavement Support
bereavement.body: Bereavement support is not an add-on to hospice — it is part of hospice. Surviving family members receive support for thirteen months after the death, at no cost.
bereavement.programNote: Our bereavement program continues for thirteen months and may include phone check-ins, support-group information, mailings about coping with grief, and a contact around the anniversary of the death.

griefIs.title: Grief looks different for everyone
griefIs.body: Grief is not linear and does not follow a schedule. You may feel waves of sadness, relief, gratitude, anger, numbness, or sudden tears at unexpected moments. All of this is normal. Be patient with yourself.

whenToSeekHelp.title: When to seek additional help
whenToSeekHelp.body: Reach out to a counselor or a primary care doctor — and let our bereavement coordinator know — if you experience prolonged inability to function, hopelessness that does not lift, persistent insomnia, or any thoughts of harming yourself.
whenToSeekHelp.lifelineLabel: If you or someone you know is in crisis, call or text:
whenToSeekHelp.lifelineNumber: 988 — Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (call or text, free, 24/7)

quickRef.title: Quick References
quickRef.intro: Print this page (use Ctrl/Cmd-P) for a portable reference. Navigation, sidebar, and bottom buttons are hidden in the printed version.

hospice.label: Hospice 24/7 nurse line
hospice.value: For symptoms, medication questions, equipment problems, any clinical change.

socialWorker.label: Hospice social worker
socialWorker.value: For emotional support, family concerns, practical-help questions, community resources.

chaplain.label: Hospice chaplain
chaplain.value: For spiritual care, end-of-life meaning, prayer or ritual, in any tradition.

lifeline.label: 988 — Suicide & Crisis Lifeline
lifeline.value: Call or text, free, 24/7. For mental-health emergencies for the caregiver or any family member.

nineEleven.label: 911 — only if revoking hospice
nineEleven.value: A 911 transport typically leads to hospital admission and curative treatment, which conflicts with the hospice election. For symptoms, call the hospice nurse line first.

items.1: Current medication list and the medication record (when each dose was given).
items.2: A short diary of new or changed symptoms since the last visit.
items.3: Questions for the nurse — written down so they are not forgotten.
items.4: Family contact list and any change in primary caregiver since the last visit.

quickRef.printNote: To print: use your browser's print function (Ctrl-P on Windows, Cmd-P on Mac). The header, sidebar, and bottom buttons will be hidden automatically.

faq.metaDescription: Answers to the most common questions families ask about hospice — getting started, what hospice includes, insurance, family caregiving, and patient rights.
faq.introParagraph: These are the questions we hear most often. Each answer is short by design; follow the links for the full picture, and call us any time if you'd like to talk through your situation.
faq.jsonLdName: Eminent Hospice Care — Frequently Asked Questions

whenConsider.a: Any time a doctor has indicated the illness is likely to progress in the next six months. Earlier referral usually means better symptom control and more time with the team — hospice is not only for the last few days. See Understanding Hospice for fuller detail.

howStart.q: How do we start hospice care?
howStart.a: Call our 24/7 line or use the Contact form. We coordinate with the attending physician to certify eligibility, and an RN can typically visit within 48 hours. See Hospice Services → How to Start.

howQuickly.a: In most cases, within 48 hours of the referral. Urgent situations can sometimes be admitted the same day.

keepDoctor.q: Can my loved one keep their own doctor?
keepDoctor.a: Yes. The patient names the attending physician on the hospice election. The hospice medical director coordinates with that doctor.

ifImprove.q: What if our loved one improves and no longer needs hospice?
ifImprove.a: Patients can revoke the hospice election at any time and resume curative care. They can re-elect hospice later if needed — there is no penalty. See Insurance & Medicare → Election Process.

whatIncludes.a: Nursing care, medications related to the terminal illness, durable medical equipment, aide help, social-work and chaplain support, volunteer companionship, and thirteen months of bereavement support for the family. See Hospice Services for the full list.

whereCare.a: Wherever the patient calls home — house, apartment, assisted living, skilled nursing facility — or, when symptoms cannot be managed at home, in a contracted inpatient setting.

twentyFourSeven.a: A registered nurse is available by phone 24/7 and can come out for urgent needs. Scheduled visits happen during the day; the family or a private caregiver is typically present overnight unless the patient is at a higher level of care.

sedated.q: Will my loved one be sedated or unconscious?
sedated.a: The goal is comfort, not sedation. Medications are titrated to the symptom; many patients remain alert and responsive for much of their hospice stay.

hastens.a: No. Studies consistently show that hospice patients often live as long as or longer than non-hospice patients with the same diagnoses, because symptoms are well managed. See Understanding Hospice → Myths vs. Facts.

cost.a: For most patients, the out-of-pocket cost is $0. Medicare Part A and Medi-Cal cover the full hospice benefit when elected; small co-pays may apply for some outpatient drugs (up to $5) and respite care. See Insurance & Medicare.

medicare.a: Yes — Medicare Part A covers the full hospice benefit when the patient is certified terminally ill and elects hospice. See Insurance & Medicare → Medicare Part A.

medical.a: Yes. Medi-Cal covers hospice services and, for patients in a nursing facility, pays for room and board at at least 95% of the facility rate. See Insurance & Medicare → Medi-Cal.

private.a: Most private insurers cover hospice; benefits vary. Our admissions team verifies coverage at no cost. See Insurance & Medicare → Comparison.

uninsured.q: What if we don't have insurance?
uninsured.a: Call us. We help families navigate Medicare and Medi-Cal eligibility and discuss options for those who qualify for neither.

familyRole.q: What does the family have to do?
familyRole.a: The family is the primary caregiver in most home-hospice settings, with our team supporting through visits, training, and 24/7 phone access. We teach what you need to know; you are not alone. See For Families & Caregivers for the practical basics.

cantCareAtHome.q: What if we cannot care for our loved one at home?
cantCareAtHome.a: Options include arranging more aide or private-caregiver hours, transferring to an assisted living or skilled nursing facility (hospice continues), or moving to a higher level of inpatient care when clinically appropriate. See Hospice Services → Levels of Care.

bereavement.a: Yes — for thirteen months after the death, at no cost. It may include phone check-ins, support-group information, and a contact around the anniversary. See For Families → Bereavement.

cultural.q: Will cultural or religious traditions be respected?
cultural.a: Yes. Our chaplain coordinates with the family's tradition, and the care plan accommodates dietary, prayer, and end-of-life rituals.

korean.a: Yes. Korean-speaking team members are available for admissions and care, and key written materials are available in Korean. See About → Care for Korean-American Families.

stopAnytime.q: Can my loved one stop hospice at any time?
stopAnytime.a: Yes. The patient can revoke the hospice election at any time and resume curative care. They can re-elect hospice later. See Insurance & Medicare → Election Process.

disagreeCarePlan.q: What if I disagree with the care plan?
disagreeCarePlan.a: The care plan is yours and the patient's to shape. Talk to the RN case manager; if concerns persist, ask for the hospice medical director or file a grievance. See Hospice Laws → Patient Rights.

complaint.q: How do we file a complaint?
complaint.a: Three paths: directly with our office, with the California Department of Public Health Licensing & Certification at (800) 228-1019, or with the Medicare Beneficiary Ombudsman at 1-800-MEDICARE. Filing cannot result in retaliation. See Hospice Laws → Complaints.

palliative.q: What is the difference between hospice and palliative care?
palliative.a: Palliative care focuses on symptom relief at any stage of serious illness and can be received alongside curative treatment. Hospice is palliative care for a terminal illness with a prognosis of six months or less, and replaces curative treatment for that illness.

advanceDirective.a: A written document — typically a Living Will and/or Durable Power of Attorney for Healthcare — that tells your care team what you want if you cannot speak for yourself. California uses the Advance Health Care Directive form. See Hospice Laws → Advance Directives.

contact.metaDescription: Speak with our care team — available 24/7. Call, email, or send us a message and we will reply within one business day.
contact.introParagraph: Have a question or want to speak with our care team? Call us 24/7 at {phone}, or send a message below — we typically reply within one business day.

form.heading: Send us a message
form.phoneHint: We will only call if email is unreachable.

info.heading: Reach us directly
info.hoursValue: Available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week

success.title: Thank you
success.body: We have received your message and will be in touch within one business day. For anything urgent, please call us at {phone}.

referral.metaDescription: Refer a patient to Eminent Hospice Care. Call our admissions line 24/7 or request a callback at a time that works for you.
referral.introParagraph: Referring a patient to hospice should be the easiest part of a hard day. Call our admissions line 24/7, or share a callback time below and we will reach out.

phoneCta.title: Call to refer a patient — 24/7
phoneCta.subtitle: An admissions nurse answers every call.

whyPhone.title: Why phone, not a web form?
whyPhone.body: Sharing patient health information online requires extra protections we are currently finalizing. In the meantime, please call us so we can take the referral directly — it is the fastest path.

callbackForm.title: Or schedule a callback
callbackForm.intro: Share the best way and time to reach you. We do not collect any patient information on this form.
callbackForm.emailHint: We will use this only if we cannot reach you by phone.

modeA.privacyNote: We are not collecting any patient health information on this form. We will call you to take the referral by phone.

trustBadges.baa: Business Associate Agreement with our email vendor

fullForm.intro: All fields below are transmitted securely. Required fields are marked.
fullForm.notesHint: Anything that will help our admissions team prepare.

modeB.privacyNote: We treat this submission as protected health information (PHI) under HIPAA. It is transmitted encrypted to our admissions team via a vendor with whom we have signed a Business Associate Agreement (BAA). Submissions are retained for {days} days, then deleted.

modeA.title: Thank you
modeA.body: We have received your callback request. A care coordinator will call within one business day. For anything urgent, please call us at {phone}.

modeB.title: Referral received
modeB.body: The referral has been securely transmitted to our admissions team. A coordinator will follow up within 24 hours. We have not sent a copy of this submission to your email — for your privacy, please retain your own records.

meta.ogDescription: Compassionate, culturally competent hospice care serving North Hollywood and the greater Los Angeles area, including the Korean-American community. Available 24/7 in English and Korean.
meta.ogImageAlt: Eminent Hospice Care — compassionate hospice care in Los Angeles

notFound.metaDescription: The page you were looking for does not exist.
notFound.heading: We can't find that page
notFound.body: The page you tried to reach may have moved or no longer exists. Return to our homepage, browse the navigation above, or call us at {phone} — we're available 24/7.

error.heading: Something went wrong
error.body: An unexpected error occurred while loading this page. Please try again. If this persists, please call us at {phone} — we are available 24 hours a day.

privacy.metaDescription: How Eminent Hospice Care collects, uses, and protects the personal information you share with us through this website.
privacy.introParagraph: This Privacy Policy explains what information we collect when you visit our website or submit a form, how we use it, who we share it with, and how long we keep it. We aim for clarity over legalese.

overview.title: Overview
overview.body: Eminent Hospice Care Inc. operates this website to provide information about hospice services and to receive inquiries and patient referrals. We collect only the information you submit through our forms, and we use it only to respond to you or coordinate care. We do not sell your personal information, we do not run advertising trackers, and we do not use cookies on this site.

dataCollected.title: Information we collect
dataCollected.intro: We collect personal information only when you submit it directly through one of our forms.

contactForm.title: Contact form
contactForm.body: When you submit our Contact form, we collect your name, email address, phone number (optional), the subject category you selected, and the message you wrote.

referralFormModeA.title: Referral form — callback request
referralFormModeA.body: When you request a callback through our Referral page (the default mode), we collect only the referrer's name, phone number, optional email, preferred callback time, and referrer type (physician, family member, case manager, or other). We do not collect any patient health information through this form. Patient information is taken by phone.

referralFormModeB.title: Referral form — full intake (when enabled)
referralFormModeB.body: When the full Referral intake is enabled, we collect patient identifiers (legal name, date of birth, primary diagnosis, current location, preferred language), insurance type and member ID, urgency, the referring physician's name and NPI, and any notes. This information is Protected Health Information (PHI) under HIPAA and is transmitted only over encrypted channels through a vendor with whom we have a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA).

serverLogs.title: Server logs
serverLogs.body: Our server records standard request metadata (timestamp, IP address hashed daily, opaque request ID, HTTP status). We do not log the body of any form submission. Logs are retained only for operational and security purposes.

cookies.title: Cookies and tracking
cookies.body: We do not use cookies on this website. We do not embed third-party analytics, advertising, or social-media tracking pixels. Cloudflare Turnstile, used to prevent bot submissions, runs without cookies in its invisible mode.

howWeUse.title: How we use your information
howWeUse.body: We use the information you submit only to:
bullets: Respond to your inquiry or schedule a callback.
Coordinate patient care and admissions when you make a referral.
Operate, secure, and improve this website.

howWeShare.title: How we share your information
howWeShare.body: We share your form submission only with the vendors required to deliver it to us, and only under contracts that bind them to confidentiality and (for protected health information) HIPAA. Our email delivery vendor transmits your submission to our staff. Our hosting provider routes the network traffic. We do not sell or rent your information to anyone, and we do not share it for advertising purposes.

retention.title: How long we keep your information
retention.body: Submissions are retained at our email vendor for approximately 90 days, and in our staff mailboxes for up to 90 days, after which they are deleted. Server logs follow standard operational retention. If you ask us to delete your information sooner, we will do so unless we are required by law or by your hospice care plan to retain it.

security.title: How we protect your information
security.body: All form submissions are transmitted over TLS 1.2 or higher. Our HIPAA-eligible email vendor encrypts submissions at rest. We restrict access to your information on a need-to-know basis. We do not store form submissions in any application database, and we never log the contents of a submission — only request metadata.

ccpa.title: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA / CPRA)
ccpa.body: If you are a California resident, you have the right to know what personal information we have collected about you, to request that we delete it, to correct inaccurate information, and to be free from discrimination for exercising these rights. We do not sell or share your personal information for cross-context behavioral advertising, so there is no "Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information" link on this site.
ccpa.howToExercise: To exercise any of these rights, email us at eminenthospice@gmail.com or call us at {phone}. We may ask for limited information to verify your identity before we act on your request.

hipaa.title: HIPAA
hipaa.body: If you are a patient or the personal representative of a patient, you have additional rights under HIPAA — including the right to access, amend, and receive an accounting of disclosures of your protected health information. Those rights are described in our HIPAA Notice of Privacy Practices.

children.title: Children
children.body: This website is not directed to children under the age of 13. We do not knowingly collect personal information from children. If you believe a child has submitted personal information to us, please contact us and we will delete it.

changes.title: Changes to this policy
changes.body: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. The "Last reviewed" date at the top reflects the most recent revision. Material changes will be noted on this page.

contact.title: Contact us about privacy
contact.body: If you have questions about this Privacy Policy or how we handle your information, please contact us:

hipaaNotice.metaDescription: Our Notice of Privacy Practices under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA).
hipaaNotice.introParagraph: This page is the home of our HIPAA Notice of Privacy Practices. The full Notice is being finalized; in the meantime, please call us to request a current copy.

overview.title: About this Notice
overview.body: A HIPAA Notice of Privacy Practices is a document that all health care providers are required by federal law to make available to patients. It describes how the provider may use and disclose protected health information (PHI), explains the patient's rights under HIPAA, and tells the patient how to file a complaint if those rights are violated.

interim.title: Notice in finalization
interim.body: We are finalizing our Notice of Privacy Practices. While we complete that document, please call us at {phone} to request a current copy of our Notice. We will provide it by mail, email, or in person, whichever you prefer. This page will be updated with the full text once the Notice is published.

accessibility.metaDescription: Our commitment to making this website accessible to people with disabilities, our current conformance status, and how to reach us if you encounter a barrier.
accessibility.introParagraph: We are committed to making this website accessible to the widest possible audience, including people with disabilities. This statement describes what we have built, what we know we still need to do, and how to contact us if you encounter a barrier.

commitment.title: Our commitment
commitment.body: Accessibility is part of how we treat patients and families with dignity. We design this website to be navigable by keyboard, readable by screen readers, and visible to people with low vision.

conformance.title: Conformance status
conformance.body: This website aims to conform to the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 at Level AA. We currently consider this website to be partially conformant, meaning that some content does not yet fully meet the accessibility standard.

features.title: Accessibility features in place
items: A "skip to main content" link at the top of every page.
Full keyboard navigation — every interactive element is reachable by Tab and operable by Enter or Space.
Visible focus indicators on every interactive element.
Form labels associated with every input; errors announced to screen readers.
Bilingual content (English and Korean) with a clearly labeled language toggle.
Color contrast ratios meeting WCAG AA: at least 4.5:1 for body text and 3:1 for large text and UI components.
Touch targets of at least 44 by 44 pixels on interactive elements.
Semantic HTML — proper headings, landmarks, lists, tables, and labels.

knownIssues.title: Known limitations
knownIssues.intro: We are aware of these issues and are working to address them:
items: Some illustrative imagery does not yet have detailed text descriptions; we are reviewing each in turn.
We have not yet completed a third-party accessibility audit. We plan to commission one before our next major redesign.

howToReport.title: How to report an accessibility issue
howToReport.body: If you encounter a barrier on this website, please tell us. We want to fix it.
howToReport.responseTime: We aim to respond within five business days.

terms.metaDescription: The terms that govern your use of this website. These are not a service contract for hospice care.
terms.introParagraph: These Terms of Use govern your access to and use of this website. By using this site, you agree to these terms. These terms are not a service contract for hospice care.

acceptance.title: Acceptance
acceptance.body: By accessing or using this website, you agree to these Terms of Use and our Privacy Policy. If you do not agree, please do not use the site.

useOfSite.title: Use of the site
useOfSite.intro: This site is provided for informational purposes about Eminent Hospice Care and hospice care generally. You may:
bullets: Read, share, and refer to the information on these pages.
Submit a contact or referral form when you want to reach our care team.
Print or save copies for your personal, non-commercial reference.

notMedicalAdvice.title: Not medical advice
notMedicalAdvice.body: The information on this site is general education, not medical, legal, or insurance advice. It is not a substitute for personalized guidance from your physician, hospice nurse, attorney, or insurance carrier. Always consult a qualified professional for your specific situation.

notServiceContract.title: Submitting a form does not create a relationship
notServiceContract.body: Submitting a contact form, a referral, or a callback request does not by itself establish a hospice care relationship with Eminent Hospice Care. A hospice care relationship begins only when admission paperwork is signed and a plan of care is in place.

intellectualProperty.title: Intellectual property
intellectualProperty.body: The content on this site — text, images, design, and code — is owned by Eminent Hospice Care Inc. or its licensors and is protected by copyright and other laws. You may not reproduce or republish substantial portions without our written permission. Educational and personal use is permitted.

externalLinks.title: Links to other websites
externalLinks.body: Where we link to outside websites (such as Medicare.gov or the California Department of Public Health), we are not responsible for the accuracy, content, or privacy practices of those sites.

disclaimers.title: Disclaimers
disclaimers.body: This website is provided "as is" and "as available." To the fullest extent permitted by California law, we disclaim all warranties — express or implied — including warranties of accuracy, fitness for a particular purpose, and non-infringement. We do not warrant that the site will be uninterrupted or error-free.

limitationOfLiability.title: Limitation of liability
limitationOfLiability.body: To the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, Eminent Hospice Care Inc. is not liable for any indirect, incidental, special, or consequential damages arising from your use of this website.

governingLaw.title: Governing law
governingLaw.body: These Terms of Use are governed by the laws of the State of California, without regard to its conflict-of-law principles. Any dispute will be resolved in the state or federal courts located in Los Angeles County, California.

changes.title: Changes to these terms
changes.body: We may update these Terms of Use from time to time. The "Last reviewed" date at the top reflects the most recent revision. Continued use of the site after a change constitutes acceptance of the updated terms.

contact.title: Contact us
contact.body: If you have questions about these terms, please contact us:

griefSupport.metaDescription: Eminent Hospice provides thirteen months of bereavement support to surviving family members at no cost — phone check-ins, support-group resources, anniversary contacts, and crisis guidance.
griefSupport.introParagraph: When someone you love dies, the work of hospice does not end. Our bereavement program supports surviving family members for thirteen months after the loss — at no cost, and with no referral needed.

program.title: Our 13-Month Bereavement Program
program.intro: Bereavement support is not an add-on to hospice — it is part of hospice. Every family we serve is automatically enrolled.

coordinator.title: Your Bereavement Coordinator
coordinator.body: Our bereavement coordinator stays in touch with surviving family for thirteen months after the loss — phone calls, support-group resources, and a contact around the anniversary of the death. You do not need to reach out first; we will call you.

cta.title: We are here for you
cta.body: If you are a family member of a patient we served and would like to connect with our bereavement coordinator, please call or send us a message.
